title: How Planets Build Moon Systems Through Accretion, Impact, and Capture

imageCredit.creator: Original work: Citronade; French translation: Charlestpt

lesson.learn:
Moons can form around planets in several different ways, and no single mechanism explains all of them. The most familiar path is accretion in a circumplanetary disk, where dust and rock orbit a young planet and gradually stick together into larger bodies. This is similar to how planets form in a disk around a star, but on a smaller scale. A second path is the giant impact hypothesis, where a large collision throws material into orbit and that debris later settles into one or more moons. Earth's Moon is the classic example.

A third path is capture. Sometimes a passing object becomes trapped by a planet's gravity and remains in orbit. Many irregular moons have tilted or eccentric paths that make capture the best explanation. Once moons are in place, they can influence a planet for billions of years by driving tides, stabilizing tilt, and even heating interiors through gravitational flexing.

Moon systems are therefore not just leftovers from formation. They are part of the active architecture of a planetary system, shaping geology, climate, and sometimes habitability long after the planet itself forms.
lesson.simpler: Moons can form when material around a young planet sticks together, when a giant impact throws debris into orbit, or when gravity captures a passing body. After that, moons can affect tides, tilt, and even internal heating on the planet.
lesson.keyTakeaway: Moons usually form by accretion, impact, or capture, and once they exist they can strongly influence the planets they orbit.

learnImageCredit.creator: National Aeronautics and Space Administration / Jet Propulsion Laboratory / Malin Space Science Systems

thirdImageCredit.creator: NASA, ESA, G. Schneider (University of Arizona), and the HST/GO 12228 Team

lesson.deeperDive:
Accretion is the most orderly moon-forming process. Material in a disk can collide and stick, producing regular moons that orbit in the same direction as the planet's rotation. That is why many giant-planet moons are arranged in neat families. Giant impacts are messier but can create large, stable moons if enough debris is thrown into orbit and not immediately lost. Earth's Moon remains the most important case because it reshaped how scientists think about the early Earth.

Capture explains the irregular moons. Their odd orbits often point to a history of gravitational trapping rather than local birth. Some captured moons later interact tidally with the planet and settle into more stable configurations, while others remain tilted and distant. Once moons exist, they become active agents. Io is volcanically driven by tidal heating, Europa and Enceladus may harbor subsurface oceans, and Earth's Moon stabilizes our axial tilt.

This is why moon formation matters beyond astronomy. Moon systems affect climates, tides, geology, and even the long-term conditions for life on a planet's surface.
lesson.funFact: Earth's Moon is unusually large compared with the planet it orbits, which is one reason the Earth-Moon pair is sometimes treated almost like a double system.
references: Planetary science texts on moon formation and circumplanetary disks.

people: William Herschel - expanded knowledge of planetary satellites
Pierre-Simon Laplace - early celestial mechanics frameworks
Galileo Galilei - first observed moons orbiting another planet
Carl Sagan - popularized planetary science concepts
places: The Moon - Earth's natural satellite and giant-impact reference point
Jupiter's moon system - diverse example of regular and irregular moons
Ganymede - largest moon in the Solar System
Europa - key astrobiology target with a subsurface ocean
years: 1979 - Voyager flybys reveal detailed moon structures
2000s-present - missions refine moon formation and tidal heating models
concepts: Accretion - growth by collision and sticking
Giant impact hypothesis - moon formation through collision debris
Gravitational capture - trapping a passing object into orbit
Circumplanetary disk - moon-forming disk around a young planet
Tidal heating - internal heating from gravitational flexing
Orbital resonance - repeating gravitational interaction between bodies
